fields.caption: X revised (for nodes)
fields.tableau_formula:
IF [type] = "node" 
    AND NOT  ISNULL([Usage])
    AND [path] = 0

    THEN ([Usage] / {MAX([Usage])} ) * [Calculation_1744441189486841860]

END

fields.caption: X fixed for last path
fields.tableau_formula:
IF [path] = { FIXED [id]: MAX(If [type] = "link" THEN [path] ELSE NULL END)}
   
    THEN ([Usage ration] * [Calculation_1744441189486841860]) + [Calculation_1744441189486841860]

ELSE [Calculation_1744441189486841860] 

END

fields.caption: Calculation1
fields.tableau_formula:
if [type] = "node" 

    AND ISNULL([Calculation_1093530316342710277])

    THEN { FIXED [id]: AVG([Usage])} 

END

fields.caption: Elec gen at 2022
fields.tableau_formula: IF  [Year] = 2022 THEN [Per capita electricity - kWh] END

fields.caption: Max year
fields.tableau_formula: if WINDOW_max(MAX([Year])) = MAX([Year]) THEN SUM([Per capita electricity - kWh]) END

fields.caption: Consumption Axis Test
fields.tableau_formula:
WINDOW_MIN(
    IF (WINDOW_MIN(MIN([Calculation_1093530317043372187])) = MIN([Calculation_1093530317043372187])) 
    THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)

fields.caption: Y Axis Position
fields.tableau_formula:
if SIZE()=1 THEN 0.5
ELSEIF SIZE() <>1 and [Calculation_1093530317043937438] = 1 THEN [Calculation_1093530317044252835]
END

fields.caption: Normalised consumption
fields.tableau_formula:
//sum(consumption) / window_max(sum(consumption)

(SUM([Per capita electricity - kWh])-WINDOW_MIN(SUM([Per capita electricity - kWh])))
/
(WINDOW_MAX(SUM([Per capita electricity - kWh]))-WINDOW_MIN(SUM([Per capita electricity - kWh])))

fields.caption: CY Consumption
fields.tableau_formula:
SUM({ FIXED [child]: sum(IF [Year]=[Calculation_1093530317044928677] THEN [Per capita electricity - kWh]
ELSE NULL END)})

fields.caption: Calculation2
fields.tableau_formula: [Calculation_1093530317044740260] > SUM({ FIXED [Region]: AVG([Per capita electricity - kWh])})

fields.caption: Links with usage
fields.tableau_formula: if [type]='link' then MAKEPOINT(-[X doubled for last path (copy)_1093530316398051351],[Calculation_1093530316395446293]) END

fields.caption: Max year (copy)
fields.tableau_formula: if [Year] = [Calculation_1093530317044928677] THEN [Per capita electricity - kWh] END

fields.caption: Nodes with usage
fields.tableau_formula: if [type]='node' then MAKEPOINT(-[X revised (copy)_1093530316345606150]-[X Normalized (copy)_1744441189486952453],[Calculation_1093530316342710277]+[Calculation_1744441189486841860]) END

fields.caption: Y fixed for last path
fields.tableau_formula:
IF [path] = { FIXED [id]: MAX(If [type] = "link" THEN [path] ELSE NULL END)}
   
    THEN ([Usage ration] * [X Normalized (copy)_1744441189486952453]) + [X Normalized (copy)_1744441189486952453]

ELSE [X Normalized (copy)_1744441189486952453] 

END

fields.caption: Y revised (for nodes)
fields.tableau_formula:
IF [type] = "node" 
    
AND NOT  ISNULL([Usage])
    AND [path] = 0
    
    THEN ([Usage] / {MAX([Usage])} ) * [X Normalized (copy)_1744441189486952453]

END

children.text_html:
Exploring data on where our electricity comes from.Æ
A look into how the generation trends are changing.

Data sourced from: Our World in Data. Æ
Generation data up to 2022 was used only.Æ
Regions defined as per World Bank's classifications.

The World’s Electricity TapestryÆ :Æ Æ
Æ Æ
Exploring the Electricity profile of each country.Æ
A dive into country specific Electricity mix - unveiling how much is generated per capita, and the generation sources.Æ

1.Æ
Although the terms “electricity” and “energy” are often used interchangeably, it's important to understand that electricity is just one component of total energy consumption.

2.Æ
Comparisons of electricity generation often reflect population size (i.e. generation increases due to population rise). It does not tell us about how much electricity the average person in a given country consumes relative to another.

3.Æ
This is why the per capita electricity generation - i.e., per person, should also be considered. The largest producers — Iceland, Norway, USA and Canada — generate hundreds of times as much electricity as the smallest.

Electricity generation Æ
per capita as at 2022:Æ
Æ Æ
Each country's generation figures, grouped by region.Æ

On the visualisation to the right, we see the differences in average electricity generation per capita across the world.Æ

Clearly, there are large inequalities between countries. Æ

In many low-income countries, per capita electricity generation is several magnitudes lower than in the richest countries.Æ

This is magnified at a regional level as well.Æ

Hover over each country to view historic trends.

For reference below: Each region's average generation in 2022 (as shown by the blue bars). For comparison, the black line represents the World average generation (3,616.7 kWh / capita).Æ

There is still a large disparity in the amount of electricity generated per person. Æ
Population growth means that there are some regions where populations still struggle with access to electricity, despite progress being made over the years.

So, what are the sources of electricity generation? Æ
And how much do they contribute? A look into their profile over the years.

Electricity sources can be broken into 3 main groups: Fossil Fuels, Nuclear and Renewables. Æ
Fossil Fuels have been hostorically the dominant source of electricity production. This includes coal, gas and oil. Renewables include bioenergy, hydro, solar, wind, and others (such as wave and tidal).

FOSSIL FUELSÆ
Æ Æ
Fossil fuels are the sum of coal, oil, and gas. Combined, they are the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions.Æ
Æ Æ
The majority of electricity is still generated from fossil fuels, predominantly coal and gas.

NUCLEARÆ
Æ Æ
Nuclear energy is one of the oldest low carbon energy technologies.  Æ
Æ Æ
Almost 10% of the global electricity is nuclear generated.Æ
Some countries, such as Sweden and France rely heavily on it.Æ

RENEWABLESÆ
Æ Æ
Almost 30% of the global electricity is from renewable sources. Some countries mainly get majority of their electricity from renewables.Æ
Æ Æ
Developing countries are some of the biggest adapters. Solar, wind, and other technologies are growing quickly overall.

Electricity generation by Country (per capita) & by source: Year on year trendsÆ
Æ Æ
Now, we can look at the electricity generation profiles of various countries, based on these three crucial sources. Æ
Each grid contains a stacked area chart for each country. They show the percentage of generation per source over the years. Æ

Generation figures (kWh / capita) are also provided for each source. Hover over each chart to view information accordingly.Æ
Data is provided until 2022, however the starting years differ for each country, depending on availability of data.

Data shown from 1985 to 2022.Æ

 For easy guidance, you can enterÆ
 a year to set the reference lineÆ
 per country.
Visualisations created by: Rushil Chhibber |Æ https://public.tableau.com/app/profile/rush1056Æ | Data from: Our World in Data | Regions defined as per World Bank's classifications.

limitations_encountered.item: viz.sankey (ds.tree spatial fields: Nodes, Links, Border, Nodes with usage, Links with usage)
limitations_encountered.issue: The dashboard's tree/Sankey diagram is drawn from 5 spatial MAKEPOINT calculated fields on the 'tree' source; a Sankey/flow diagram has NO native Power BI visual and MAKEPOINT/MAKELINE have no DAX/Power Query equivalent. The 5 spatial fields are NOT modelled. Instead the underlying normalized layout coordinates are retained as ordinary columns on the 'Tree' table (X Normalized, Y Normalized, X/Y revised (for nodes), X/Y fixed for last path, Consumption axis, Source label, Filter) so the report can drive a custom/AppSource Sankey visual or fall back to a decomposition tree / treemap. HIGH capability gap for pbi-report-builder.

The 'tree' source's tree.hyper packages TWO tables - tree.csv (3,320 geometry rows, exported to ds.tree.csv) AND 'Elec generation' (6,061 consumption rows) - but the extractor exported only the geometry table. The consumption columns the tree calcs reference ([Per capita electricity - kWh], [Year], [Region], [Entity]) are ABSENT from ds.tree.csv. They were sourced from ds.elec_generation_per_capita_regions.csv (identical data) and the tree consumption calcs (CY, CY Consumption, Max year, Calculation2, Elec gen at 2022, 'Max year (copy)') were relocated onto the 'Elec Generation' table (child=Entity). Parser/extraction gap: multi-table .hyper sources should export every referenced table, not just the first.

The '5 data sources = data blending' premise is inaccurate for this workbook: every one of the 17 worksheets is single-source and there are NO dashboard actions, so no active Tableau blend exists. The sources are alternative shapes of the same OWID electricity dataset. Mapped to 5 independent fact tables. The only genuine cross-table relationship is the tree noodle tree.csv[child] = 'Elec generation'[Entity] (authoritative from the .twb XML), modelled many-to-many. A conformed 'Year' dimension (1:many, single cross-filter to the 4 year-bearing facts) was added as the faithful translation of a 'blend on Year'.

The Country/Region 'blend keys' were intentionally NOT built into a shared geo dimension. Entity domains differ across sources (Per Capita FNR has 223 entities incl. 'World'; Elec Generation has 209 countries + 7 regions, no 'World'; Pivoted is country-grain) so a conformed geo dimension would introduce referential-integrity mismatches, and the single-source worksheets never require cross-source geo filtering. Documented as a design decision, not a defect; a report needing cross-source geo slicing would require a curated Entity/Region bridge.

Tree[Child] -> Elec Generation[Entity] is modelled many-to-many with single cross-filter (Tree filters Elec Generation), chosen so the tree-source worksheets ('Country trend', 'Advanced table', 'Advanced table no region') filter consumption by child node. Deliberate assumption: switch to bothDirections if the report needs an Elec-Generation-side slicer (e.g. Region/Income group) to also filter which tree nodes display. child is non-unique in tree.csv (link+node+border rows) and Entity is non-unique per year, hence m:m rather than a 1:many star.

limitations_encountered.item: ds.tree / ds.pivoted / ds.by_source trellis + window-layout table calcs (dropped)
limitations_encountered.issue: Tableau small-multiple/layout and window table calcs were DROPPED as they compute visual grid coordinates, not model values: X-trellis = (index()-1) % int(SQRT(size())) and Y-trllis = int((index()-1)/int(sqrt(size()))) [small multiples are a native PBI visual feature]; Rank = index(); Consumption Axis Test = WINDOW_MIN(...); Y Axis Position; Normalised consumption = window min/max normalization; and the redundant aliases Year Axis Consumption (= [Year]) and Year Axis CY (= [CY]). The report should use the visual's built-in small-multiples, rank/Top-N and axis-normalization features instead.

The 'By source' data source (caption 'Country & Regions', 5,496 rows) is bound by ZERO worksheets (verified across all 17). It is modelled as the 'By Source' table only to satisfy the explicit 5-table requirement; it is vestigial and can be dropped with no loss of report fidelity. Its % ratio calc columns were translated for completeness.

Tableau [Global avg in 2022] is the literal constant 3616.7, which equals the FNR 'World' entity's 2022 TOTAL per-capita (3616.692) - NOT the mean of the 209 country per-capita values in this source (that mean is 4074.4). Preserved as the literal 3616.7 to match the workbook's reference line; noted here so a future maintainer does not 'correct' it to a computed average.
